text: For most "stars" in the NBA it's perfectly fine to give them a pass on stinkers and to go "but his teammates..." But once you start getting the generational tag, the best in the world tag, the "should have 5 straight MVPs" tag. Once you enter the ALL TIME conversations, the criticism changes. You're required so much more, if that's who you really are. Not just the best player on the floor, but in the league and the best guy anyone on that floor may ever see. From that standpoint, what Jokic did last night is unacceptable. From that standpoint what Jokic has failed to achieve to this point is unacceptable. So either 1) He is an All Time great and has failed at that level or 2) He's not really and All Time great, just a Big Man that plays PG and racks up stats, but shouldn't hold the responsibility of an all time great. We're at the point with Jokic that it has to be one or the other but many in media and Jokic fans are trying to have all the credit and none of the blame, and that's just not how it works
label: analysis

text: Net Rating: +11 (2nd best lineup min 150min) ORTG: 119 DRTG: 108 282 minutes (most out of any playoff lineup out of necessity) So if they were so elite why did they end up in two 7 game series and ultimately losing? Well its because they basically got destroyed anytime that any healthy starter was out. Jokic off -24 Braun off -25 Gordon off -26 Murray off -54 (big reason why he led the league in playoff minutes per game and in total minutes they were getting obliterated when he was off) Every game it was the same script the starters start off well, the first subs come in and they start losing, the starters try to close the game while not gassing out. Today was a great example of this once again happening when the Nuggets were up 11 until Russell Westbrook checked in following Peyton Watson shortly after and the Thunder immediately cut it to 5. Hopefully this kills the Jokic superman +/- narrative since Jokic had a -24 net rating when he played without Jamal Murray. This is going to be the 2nd out of the last 3 playoff runs that Jokic is a negative when he plays without Murray.(-11 in 22/23) This doesn’t mean that Jokic is dependent on Jamal but basketball is a team game and every player plays a part. The whole 1 player carrying concept has always been asinine. Jokic and Murray together without Gordon were a -19 so 2 players carrying isn’t possible either. Depth matters and like we just saw for the Nuggets 5 players isn’t enough to win a championship.
label: analysis

text: Most sources are saying that the most likely timeline for expansion franchises in Seattle and Vegas to begin play would be 2027-28. Given that this current era of the NBA has been defined by parity and the lack of super teams, how would expansion franchises do in this era? The majority of NBA expansion franchises have started off horrifically, is there any reason to believe hypothetical Vegas/Seattle franchises would be different in this era? Bonus question: what roster building strategy could an expansion franchise take to ensure that they would be competitive immediately?
label: analysis

text: Gilgeous-Alexander finished the regular season as the first Canadian to lead the NBA in scoring and the third international player to achieve the feat. He finished the season with the most 20-point games (75), 30-point games (49), 40-point games (13) and 50-point games (4). His streak of 72 consecutive games scoring at least 20 points became the longest such streak in NBA history, eclipsing Michael Jordan’s previous record. It was also Gilgeous-Alexander’s third consecutive season averaging at least 30 points, joining a list occupied by Michael Jordan, Wilt Chamberlain, Oscar Robertson, Kareem Abdul-Jabbar, James Harden and Bob McAdoo. He has now made a conference finals and probably has won MVP

text: For the most part, we can only speculate. Some teams are closer than others—Minnesota and Indiana, for example—given their chances this year and likely in the seasons to come. But looking beyond the next six years? That’s anyone’s guess. Ignoring specific players and teams, the odds of being the last championship-less franchise will only get tougher. Right now, it’s a 1-in-3 situation, but as the list narrows, the final team will face a daunting 1-in-30 scenario. If the league maintains its current format for the next century, there’s no guarantee every team will win a title. Expansion could shake things up—maybe two new franchises join the mix—but in our lifetimes, it’s unlikely we’ll see every team crowned. So the real question is… **who will be the last team standing?**
label: analysis

text: Since 1980, there have been only five West finals where neither team was neither a California team or a Texas team. 1990: Portland Trailblazers defeat Phoenix Suns in 6 to earn their first Finals appearance since 1977. 1992: Portland Trailblazers defeat Utah Jazz in 6. 1993: Phoenix Suns defeat Seattle Supersonics in 6 to earn their first Finals appearance since 1976. 1996: Seattle Supersonics defeat Utah Jazz in 7 to earn their first Finals appearance since 1979. 2025: Oklahoma City vs. Minnesota Timberwolves From 1980-2024, there have been only 9 West champions that were not a California team or a Texas team. 1990, 1992: Portland Trailblazers 1993, 2021: Phoenix Suns 1996: Seattle Supersonics 1997, 1998: Utah Jazz 2012: Oklahoma City Thunder 2023: Denver Nuggets Of these 9 West champions, 2023 Denver Nuggets were the only one to win the NBA Championship.

text: ## Series Breakdown: OKC vs. MIN — My Take **Boys**, I wanna hear some analysis on this series, here’s my take: --- ### My Prediction **OKC defeats MIN in 6 games.** I personally think Denver was a tougher matchup for OKC. Simply put: Jokic can stretch the defense and dominate the paint against almost anyone (except maybe Hartenstein). Aaron Gordon also presents matchup problems with his interior presence and physicality. That said, I think the length, depth, and athleticism of the Timberwolves could pose a different kind of challenge. I'm genuinely curious to hear other people's takeaways from watching their previous matchups — what stood out to you? Both OKC and MIN showed similar styles in their respective Game 7 wins over Denver. They applied constant pressure on defense, forced turnovers, got out in transition, and wore the Nuggets down with intensity and tempo. Honestly, I think the NBA is starting to feel more like the NHL — younger teams are peaking sooner, and the pace and space era makes it tougher for older, more methodical teams to keep up. Let me know if that sounds crazy to you, but that’s just how I see it. --- ### Regular Season Matchups Here’s what I found from the 2025 head-to-heads: - **Feb 13** – OKC @ MIN: OKC lost 101–116 - **Feb 23** – OKC @ MIN: OKC won 130–123 - **Feb 24** – MIN @ OKC: MIN won 131–128 - **Dec 31** – MIN @ OKC: OKC won 113–105 Series tied 2–2 in the regular season. ### Inactive Players by Game #### **24-Feb** > *No Gobert, no Randle, n

text: If the goalpost keeps getting moved like it does currently. A ring is "only one ring" now. Outside of IDK, Hakeem rings, the pistons in 2004 and the Mavs in 2011 no other ring should count because every star player had either a stacked team or beat "weak"/injured teams in the finals. Bird had McHale and Parish. Kareem and Magic had each other and a couple more hall of famers. MJ had Pippen, Rodman and a couple other hall of famers Duncan had Ginobili, Robinson, Parker and Kawhi. Shaq and Kobe had each other. Toronto beat the injured Warrios and so on. Every single title run can have an asterisk put onto them. Enjoy when your team wins and don't be petty when it loses. Also don't be petty when it wins, that's even worse.

text: Dating back to 2019, the last 6 NBA championships have been won by 6 different team. With the Nuggets, Warriors, and Celtics losing in the second round, the 2025 championship will be won by a different team as well. For the first time in NBA history, there will be 7 different champions in a 7 year span: - 2019 Champion: Raptors - 2020 Champion: Lakers - 2021 Champion: Bucks - 2022 Champion: Warriors - 2023 Champion: Nuggets - 2024 Champion: Celtics - 2025 Champion: It will be the Pacers, Knicks, Timberwolves, or Thunder [Looking through all the other champions in NBA history](https://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_NBA_champions), there is only one instance that came close to this (6 different champions in 6 years): - 1975 Champion: Warriors - 1976 Champion: Celtics - 1977 Champion: Blazers - 1978 Champion: Bullets - 1979 Champion: Supersonics - 1980 Champion: Lakers It appears that the NBA has successfully achieved its goal of eliminating dynasties. By the metric of championships, this is the most parity that the league has had in its history.
label: analysis

text: Jokic is one of the greatest offensive players of this era and maybe of all time. As a Center, he bends the offensive halfcourt with his incredible touch around the rim, his masterful passing and diverse post game. On defense, he is decent in space and but provides minimal rim protection as a Center. Having him on your team guarantees you a Top 10 offense, and limits your defensive ceiling. Rudy Gobert is one of the greatest defensive players of this era and maybe of all time. He is not great in space but provides all time great rim protection. On offense, he has some of the worst hands of any active player, constantly fumbling the ball. He has terrible touch at the rim and is on offense the worst 7 foot post players you will ever see in your life. Having him on your team guarantees you a Top 10 defense and limits your offensive ceiling. Jokic has 3 MVPs Rudy has 4 DPOY Obviously I’m not trying to say these players have similar value. Just an observation of their juxtaposition as elite one way players that have underperformed in the playoffs. With Rudy having his best chance at a Championship this year, it’s possible these two will have had comporable success in this era (strictly based on how their 1 way impact contributes to win %). Is it possible Jokic is overrated, Rudy is underrated or both?
label: analysis

text: 1. 70-Game Season Starting December 1 Start the season December 1. Make Christmas Day the official league launch. Cutting 12 games improves player health, game quality, and keeps stars on the floor. 2. Designated Viewing Nights, Like College Football Move away from daily games. Focus on high-impact nights: • Monday: Rivalry Night • Wednesday: National TV Matchups • Friday: West Coast After Dark • Saturday: All-Day Showcase • Sunday: Playoff Implications NBA should be able to flex for the week similar to the NFL for the Sunday night game. 3. MLK Day = Full-League Celebration All 30 teams play. All games during the day. Tie in civil rights tributes, historic uniforms, and local storytelling. 4. NBA Cup Week – Final Week of January Top 8 teams compete in a best-of-3 FIBA-style tournament overseas. New rules, global spotlight, full-week event. Owners benefit through international media rights, sponsors, and market exposure — replacing lost revenue from a shorter regular season. All other teams get a true bye week. 5. All-Star Weekend Before March Madness Hold it the weekend before the NCAA Tournament tips off. Then shut down until the Monday after March Madness’ opening weekend. Players rest, fans recharge, anticipation builds. 6. Playoffs Start Memorial Day Weekend Launch postseason on a national holiday. Big window. Big audience. 7. NBA Finals Wrap Late July No NFL. No college. Just NBA. Take over the summer.

text: They're inconsistent as hell because there is an art to being PG and getting your teammates involved and going. Jokic hasn't figured that part out at the level required Example: Game 5, 3rd Q, Jokic and Murray start running their 2 man game after the half. It's working, and they run it the entire 3rd Q. Nobody shoots but Murray and Jokic. Looks great because it's working. The truth is OKC was fine with them running it, and in the 4th they adjusted, And suddenly it's not there anymore, and now everyone else who hasn't touched the ball since the 2nd Q now has to start hitting shots cold. And the result is the result and a direct result on what happened in the 3rd Q. Jokic has the ball every possession and everyone else is designed to play off of that, and he makes the decision. He makes the decision to find the most open guy often, but that's not the actual key, the key is finding the right guys the right shots, not just any guy any shot. It's hard to play when you're Braun and are hot early and then don't see the ball for another quarter and a half. Or MPJ never gets a chance to get in a rhythm because he gets 4 shots spaced out over 2 quarters. You can't then expect shooters to go from 0 to 100 in crunch time when they've been bystanders up until that point. Good teams can manipulate it too. They know "Jokic is going to find the most open man, so let's choose who that is" and often it'd be someone like Westbrook, intentionally, and as a PG you have to realize that a wife open

text: Set aside whether or not one views Michael Porter Jr. and his contract as a slight overpay or an albatross: The Denver Nuggets have so few assets they can attach to him or their other overpaid player in Zeke Nnaji. While they do own their 1sts in 2026, 2028, 2030-32, due to the Stepien Rule they can't trade their even-numbered picks as they owe first-round draft capital to the Thunder in 2027 & 2029. In essence, their pool of assets that wouldn't be considered untouchable for a non-star trade i.e. you aren't trading Christian Braun, is one pick in either 2031 & 2032, pick swaps for their even-numbered picks up to 2030 and the rookie DaRon Holmes who has yet to play a single game in the NBA. And no, they don't have any second round picks aside from the recently accessible 2032 2nd round pick.
label: analysis
